1: Chapter 9 is Job’s response to Bildad and marks the escalation from theological discourse to legal metaphor: Job begins to frame his situation as a lawsuit (rîḇ) against God. The courtroom vocabulary that accumulates in Job 9 — justify, answer, argue, umpire, summon — is the most sustained legal imagery in the book outside the divine speeches.
2: ûmah-yiṣdaq ʾĕnôš ʿim-ʾēl — “how can a man be justified before God?” Yiṣdaq is the niphal of ṣādaq (to be righteous/declared righteous) — a legal/forensic term, not merely a moral description. Job takes Bildad’s implicit question (Job 8:3 — does God pervert justice?) and applies it existentially: even granting that God’s justice is real, how can any creature establish righteousness in a divine court? The question is not rhetorical despair but genuine legal inquiry.
3: ʾim-yāḥpoṣ lārîḇ ʿimmô lōʾ-yaʿᵃnennû ʾaḥaṯ mittî-ʾelep — “one could not answer him once in a thousand attempts.” The legal odds — 1 in 1000 — are not pessimism but legal realism about the asymmetry between human and divine standards of proof. The same impossibility is used positively in Ecclesiastes 7:28 and Job 33:23 (if there is an angel to interpret, one among a thousand).
4: ḥᵃḵam lēḇāḇ wᵉʾammîṣ kōaḥ — “God is wise in heart and mighty in strength.” The pairing of wisdom and power is the same pairing Job celebrates in Job 12:13 (“with God are wisdom and strength”) and that Paul applies to Christ (1 Corinthians 1:24). Job’s acknowledgment of divine wisdom-and-power is not defiance but context-setting: if even Rahab’s allies are subdued by this power (Job 9:13), what chance does Job have in court?
5: hāmmַʿtîq hārîm wᵉlōʾ yāḏᵉʿû — “he removes mountains before they know it.” The suddenness of divine action — mountains overturned in anger before they are aware — echoes the cosmological power descriptions that recur through the book. The image of mountains uprooted anticipates the whirlwind speech’s questions about cosmological foundations (Job 38:4-6).
6: hammargîz ʾereṣ mimmᵉqômāh wᵉʿammûḏeḥā yiṯpallāṣûn — “who shakes the earth from its place so that its pillars quake.” The ʿammûḏîm (pillars) of the earth are cosmological elements; the image of earth-pillars quaking connects to Psalms 75:3 (I have set the pillars firm), Isaiah 24:18-20, and the theophany descriptions of divine judgment. Seismic disruption as divine action is a consistent OT theophanic motif.
7: hāʾōmēr laḥereš wᵉlōʾ yizrāḥ wᵉḇaʿᵃḏ kôḵāḇîm yaḥtōm — “who commands the sun not to rise, and seals up the stars.” The command-form (hāʾōmēr — the one who says to) emphasizes divine speech-as-command over celestial mechanics; the sun’s non-rising and the stars’ sealing (like a letter sealed with wax) represent the suspension of natural order. This is the counterpart to Genesis 1:3 (“and God said... let there be light”) — the same God who issued the creative command can revoke it.
8: nōṭeh šāmayim lᵉḇaddô wᵉdōrēḵ ʿal-bāmŏṯê yām — “who alone stretched out the heavens and walks on the crests of the sea.” Dōrēḵ ʿal-bāmŏṯê yām (walks/treads on the heights/back of the sea) is the earliest attested OT form of the divine sea-walking motif; cf. Habakkuk 3:15; Psalms 77:19. This is the conceptual precursor to Jesus walking on water (Matthew 14:25-26; John 6:19), where the disciples’ recognition — “truly you are the Son of God” — reflects the Joban theology that only God treads the sea.
9: ʿōśeh ʿāš kᵉsîl wᵉḵîmāh wᵉḥaḏrê ṯêmān — “who made the Bear, Orion, and the Pleiades.” ʿAš (the Bear/Arcturus), kᵉsîl (Orion — from the root for foolishness; the fool-constellation), and kîmāh (Pleiades) are three of the book’s five astronomical references (also in Job 38:31-32). The Creator’s authorship of the constellations is the ground of YHWH’s own challenge in Job 38:31: “Can you bind the chains of the Pleiades?” — the same constellations Job cites here as evidence of divine power.
10: ʿōśeh gᵉḏōlôṯ ʿaḏ-ʾên ḥēqer wᵉniplāʾôṯ ʿaḏ-ʾên mispār — “who does great things beyond comprehension and marvelous things without number.” This verse is nearly identical to Job 5:9 (Eliphaz’s doxology); Job is quoting Eliphaz’s own theological description of God and applying it as evidence that Job cannot win a divine lawsuit. The shared vocabulary (“unsearchable,” “without number”) shows Job mastering the Friends’ own theological categories while reaching opposite practical conclusions.
11: hēn yaʿᵃḇōr ʿālay wᵉlōʾ ʾerʾeh wᵉyaḥᵃlōp wᵉlōʾ-ʾāḇîn lô — “when he passes by me, I cannot see him; when he moves on, I cannot perceive him.” The invisibility of God in action — passing but unseen — is not atheism but a description of the divine incognito; cf. the divine presence passing before Moses (Exodus 33:22) and Elijah (1 Kings 19:11). The inability to perceive God passing is one of the existential crises of Job’s situation: God is active but not legible.
12: hēn yaḥtōp mî yᵉšîḇennû mî-yōmar ʾēlāyw māh-taaśeh — “if he snatches something away, who can stop him? Who will dare say to him, ‘What are you doing?’” The question “what are you doing?” (māh-taʿᵃśeh) echoes the question that Job wishes he could ask; it also anticipates the ironic reversal in the whirlwind when God asks Job essentially the same question — “Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth?” (Job 38:4).
13: ʾĕlôhah lōʾ-yāšîḇ ʾappô taḥtāyw šāḥᵃḥû ʿōzᵉrê Rahaḇ — “God does not restrain his anger; even the allies of Rahab crouched beneath him.” Rahaḇ here is the chaos-dragon of cosmogonic conflict (distinct from Rahab of Jericho), paralleling the Babylonian Tiamat and related to tannîn (dragon) in 3:8 and Job 26:12. The “allies of Rahab” — forces of primordial chaos — are subdued; if even cosmic chaos forces submit to God, Job has no legal recourse through power.
14: ʾap kî-ʾānōḵî ʾeʿᵃnennû ʾeḇḥᵃrāh ḏᵉḇāray ʿimmô — “how then can I answer him, or select my words to argue before him?” Eḇḥᵃrāh ḏᵉḇāray (I will choose/select my words) describes the careful rhetorical preparation of a legal advocate; Job recognizes that even if he prepares his best case, the asymmetry of the courtroom makes victory impossible on his own terms. The careful word-selection of chapters 9-10 is itself the enacted form of this preparation.
15: ʾᵃšer ʾim-ṣāḏaqtî lōʾ ʾeʿᵃneh lᵉmišpāṭî ʾetḥannān — “even if I were righteous, I could not answer him; I would have to plead for mercy to my own judge.” The legal paradox: even a righteous defendant must appeal to mercy rather than justice before this judge. ʾetḥannān (I would plead for grace/mercy) uses the same ḥānan root as Bildad’s advice in Job 8:5. Job discovers that the only possible stance before God is grace-seeking — but even this seems inadequate given God’s apparent disregard for Job’s cries.
16: ʾim-qārāʾṯî wayyaʿᵃnēnî lōʾ-ʾaʾᵃmîn kî-yaʾᵃzîn qôlî — “if I summoned him and he responded, I still could not believe he was truly listening to me.” The crisis of divine responsiveness: even a divine answer would not resolve Job’s epistemic problem, because he cannot verify that God is genuinely attending to his case. This anticipates the whirlwind response — God does answer Job, and Job’s response (Job 40:4-5; Job 42:1-6) is the unexpected form of “believing God is listening.”
17: ʾᵃšer bisᵉʿārāh yᵉšûpᵉnî wᵉhirᵉbāh pᵉṣāʿay ḥinnām — “for he would crush me with a storm and multiply my wounds for no reason.” Bisᵉʿārāh (with a storm/whirlwind) uses the exact same root as the sᵉʿārāh from which YHWH actually speaks in Job 38:1 — “out of the storm the LORD answered.” Job anticipates the divine answer will be crushing force; YHWH’s actual whirlwind-speech is confrontational but not obliterating — it is the answer that restores Job (Job 42:5-6).
18: lōʾ-yitᵉnnênî hāšēḇ rûḥî kî yaśbîʿanî mamrōrîm — “he does not let me catch my breath, but fills me with bitter suffering.” Hāšēḇ rûḥî (restore my breath/spirit) is the language of near-death recovery; Job is at the edge of being overwhelmed. Mamrōrîm (bitter things) shares a root with Naomi’s name-change request (Mārā = bitter; Ruth 1:20) — the vocabulary of suffering that has turned life to bitterness.
19: ʾim-lᵉkōaḥ ʾammiṣ hinnēh wᵉʾim-lᵉmišpāṭ mî yôʿîḏēnî — “if it is a contest of strength, he is the stronger; if it is a matter of justice, who can summon him to court?” The double impossibility: strength argument fails (God has more); justice argument fails (God cannot be summoned). The only remaining resource is the mediator/umpire Job will request in Job 9:33.
20: ʾim-ʾeṣdaq pî yaršîʿēnî tām-ʾānî wayᵉʿaqqᵉšēnî — “if I justified myself, my own mouth would condemn me; if I declared myself blameless, he would prove me crooked.” The legal trap: self-justification generates self-condemnation because the standard being applied exceeds what any creature meets. The same logic drives Paul in Romans 3:19-20 (the law silences every mouth and makes all the world accountable to God).
21: tām ʾānî lōʾ-ʾēḏaʿ napšî ʾemʾas ḥayyāy — “though I am blameless, I do not trust my own self-knowledge; I despise my own life.” Tām ʾānî (I am blameless) reaffirms Job’s integrity even as he disclaims confident self-knowledge. The epistemological humility — not knowing his own soul fully — is one of Job’s most honest moments; he is not asserting perfect self-transparency but genuine integrity based on available evidence. This anticipates Paul’s similar statement in 1 Corinthians 4:3-4.
22: ʾaḥaṯ hîʾ ʿal-kēn ʾāmartî tām wᵉrāšāʿ hûʾ mᵉkalleh — “it is all the same to me; this is why I say: he destroys the blameless and the wicked alike.” Job’s most radical claim — that divine retributive distinction is empirically absent. This is not atheism but crisis-theodicy: Job is not denying God’s existence but questioning the operational reliability of the retributive principle. The book will eventually validate Job’s integrity while not vindicating this particular claim.
23: ʾim-šôṭ yāmîṯ pitʾōm limsaṯ nᵉqiyyîm yilʿāḡ — “when sudden disaster brings death, he mocks at the calamity of the innocent.” Yilʿāḡ (mocks/laughs) applied to God is Job’s most provocative statement — yet cf. Psalms 2:4 (God laughs at human schemes) and Proverbs 1:26 (Wisdom laughs at the calamity of those who rejected her). Job’s accusation is extreme but uses vocabulary the tradition applied to God in other contexts.
24: ʾereṣ nitᵉnāh bᵉyaḏ-rāšāʿ pᵉnê-šōpᵉṭeḥā yᵉkasseh ʾim-lōʾ ʾēpôʾ mî-hûʾ — “the earth is given into the power of the wicked; he blindfolds its judges. If not he, then who?” The rhetorical question “if not he, then who?” (ʾim-lōʾ ʾēpôʾ mî-hûʾ) is Job’s form of theodicy-demand: if God is sovereign, God is responsible for the observable injustice; if God is not responsible, then who? The Friends have no answer to this question; the whirlwind does not give a direct answer either — it reframes the question.
25: wᵉyāmay qallû minnî-rāṣ — “my days are swifter than a runner.” The speed-of-life metaphors multiply: runner, reed skiffs, diving eagle — each faster than the last. The accumulation of velocity images captures the experience of time accelerating in suffering; there is no time to establish the righteousness Job claims before his life ends.
26: ḥālᵉpû ʿim-ʾonîyyôṯ ʾēḇeh kᵉneser yāṭûš ʿᵃlê-ʾōkel — “they glide away like skiffs of reed, like an eagle diving on its prey.” ʾOnîyyôṯ ʾēḇeh (boats/skiffs of papyrus/reed) — light Egyptian papyrus vessels known for speed on the Nile; the eagle diving (yāṭûš) on prey is the same eagle of Job 39:29-30; Job’s life is as fleeting as the fastest things he knows.
27: ʾim-ʾāmartî ʾeškaḥāh śîḥî ʾeʿezbāh pānay wᵉʾabliḡāh — “if I say, ‘I will forget my complaint, I will put on a cheerful face and be bright.’” Job considers the option of forced optimism — the social performance of well-being — and immediately rejects it (Job 9:28): he knows it is impossible because the underlying dread remains. The authentic lament tradition refuses cosmetic cheerfulness as a response to genuine suffering.
28: yāgōrtî kol-ʿōṣᵉḇōṯay yāḏaʿtî kî-lōʾ tᵉnaqqēnî — “I dread all my suffering, for I know you will not acquit me.” Tᵉnaqqēnî (acquit/declare innocent) is the legal term from nāqāh (to be clean/innocent); Job’s certainty of divine non-acquittal is a crisis of faith, not an absence of faith. The certainty that God will not declare him innocent despite his being innocent is the book’s central paradox until the whirlwind resolves it by declaring Job’s words “right” (Job 42:7).
29: ʾānōḵî ʾerāšaʿ lāmmāh-zeh heḇel ʾiyygāʿ — “if I am condemned already, why do I exhaust myself for nothing?” Heḇel (vapor/breath/futility) — the same word that dominates Ecclesiastes — here applies to Job’s legal effort: if the outcome is predetermined, the effort is heḇel. The word’s Ecclesiastes usage asks the same theodicy question from a different angle.
30: ʾim-hiṯrāḥaṣtî ḇᵉmê-šāleg — “even if I washed myself with snow water.” Snow water (mê šāleg) was regarded as the purest available water; the conditional sets up the maximal purity scenario. The futility of even maximum purity before God parallels Isaiah 64:6 (“all our righteous acts are like filthy rags”) — though Job’s lament is about innocence-while-condemned, not about the insufficiency of righteousness per se.
31: ʾāz baššaḥaṯ tiṭbᵉlēnî wᵉtiʿᵃḇûnî śalmôṯay — “you would still plunge me into a slime pit so that my own clothes would recoil from me.” Šaḥaṯ (pit/grave/corruption) — the same word used in Psalms 16:10 (“you will not abandon me to the realm of the dead, nor will you let your faithful one see decay”) and Jonah 2:6. The clothes recoiling from the defiled wearer — even Job’s garments reject him — captures extreme defilement imagery.
32: kî lōʾ-ʾîš kāmônî ʾeʿᵃnennû nāḇôʾ yaḥḏāw bammišpāṭ — “for he is not a man as I am, that I could answer him or that we could appear in court together.” Lōʾ-ʾîš kāmônî (not a man like me) is one of Job’s most theologically loaded statements. The asymmetry Job laments — God is not human, therefore the legal process is inaccessible — is the precise condition that the incarnation addresses: “the Word became flesh” (John 1:14) makes the divine party to the case human and therefore subject to the legal encounter Job cannot achieve.
33: lōʾ-yēš bênênû môkîaḥ yāšêṯ yāḏô ʿal-šᵉnênû — “there is no umpire between us who could place his hand upon us both.” Môkîaḥ (umpire/arbiter/one who argues/decides) is from the root yākaḥ (to argue, reprove, decide a case); the same root produces the prophetic hôkîaḥ (to rebuke/decide). The umpire who can lay hands on both God and human is the central longing of the book — and its NT answer is explicit: “For there is one God and one mediator between God and mankind, the man Christ Jesus” (1 Timothy 2:5). Job’s umpire-longing is one of the OT’s clearest anticipations of the incarnate mediator.
34: yāsēr mēʿālay šiḇṭô wᵉʾêmāṯô ʾal-tᵉḇaʿᵃtanî — “let him remove his rod from me, and let his terror not overwhelm me.” The šēḇeṭ (rod/staff) here is the instrument of divine discipline/punishment, not of comfort as in Psalms 23:4. Job is asking for the removal of both the punishment and the terror — what he wants is a hearing without the judicial pressure that makes honest speech impossible. He will eventually get this at the whirlwind: God speaks, but from 38:3 onward it is God who does the cross-examining.
35: ʾᵃḏabbēr wᵉlōʾ ʾîrāʾennû kî lōʾ-ḵēn ʾānōḵî ʿimmāḏî — “then I would speak without fear, for I am not so in myself.” Job’s condition for honest speech is removal of the terror that suppresses his words; the whirlwind encounter actually fulfills this when God commands Job: “brace yourself like a warrior; I will question you, and you will answer me” (Job 38:3; Job 40:7). The invitation to answer without annihilation is the version of Job’s request that YHWH grants.
